figures.caption: Fig. 14–1.A uniform magnetic field B\FigB in the zz-direction corresponds to a vector potential A\FigA that rotates about the zz-axis, with the magnitude A=Br′/2A=Br'/2 (r′r' is the displacement from the zz-axis).
Fig. 14–2.The vector potential A\FigA at point 11 is given by an integral over the current elements jdV\Figj\,dV at all points 22.
Fig. 14–3.A long cylindrical wire along the zz-axis with a uniform current density j\Figj.
Fig. 14–4.A long solenoid with a surface current density J\FigJ.
Fig. 14–5.A rotating charged cylinder produces a magnetic field inside. A short radial wire rotating with the cylinder has charges induced on its ends.
Fig. 14–6.A rectangular loop of wire with the current II. What is the magnetic field at PP? (R≫aR\gg a and R≫bR\gg b.)
Fig. 14–7.The distribution of jxj_x in the current loop of Fig. 14–6.
Fig. 14–8.The vector potential of a small current loop at the origin (in the xyxy-plane); a magnetic dipole field.
Fig. 14–9.For a fine wire jdV\Figj\,dV is the same as IdsI\,d\Figs.
Fig. 14–10.The magnetic field of a wire can be obtained from an integral around the circuit.
